title: Quiz: Hash-Based Signature Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of hash-based signature foundations and security properties

questions.question: What is the primary security advantage of hash-based signatures over lattice-based signatures?

options.text: Smaller signature sizes
Faster signing operations
Security relies only on well-studied hash function properties
No quantum speedup exists for attacking them

questions.explanation: Hash-based signatures have 'minimal assumptions' - their security relies solely on standard hash function properties (preimage resistance, collision resistance) that have been studied for 40+ years. Lattice-based schemes rely on newer mathematical problems like Module-LWE.

questions.question: What is preimage resistance in the context of hash functions?

options.text: Given two messages, hard to find if they have the same hash
Given a hash output h, hard to find any message m where H(m) = h
Given a message m, hard to find another message with the same hash
Hard to find any two messages with the same hash

questions.explanation: Preimage resistance means given only a hash value h, it's computationally infeasible to find any input m that hashes to h. For an n-bit hash, this requires O(2^n) operations classically.

questions.question: In a basic one-time signature (OTS), why can you only sign one message safely?

options.text: The hash function can only be used once
Each signature reveals part of the secret key
The public key changes after signing
Verification becomes slower after the first signature

questions.explanation: In OTS, signing reveals secret values corresponding to each bit of the message hash. After one signature, an attacker has seen some secrets. A second signature on a different message would reveal more secrets, potentially enough to forge signatures on new messages.

questions.question: How does Grover's algorithm affect hash-based signature security?

options.text: It breaks hash-based signatures completely
It provides exponential speedup, requiring much larger hashes
It provides quadratic speedup, requiring doubled hash output size
It has no effect on hash-based signatures

questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides a quadratic speedup for searching, reducing preimage resistance from 2^n to 2^(n/2). To maintain 128-bit post-quantum security, we use 256-bit hashes instead of 128-bit.

questions.question: What is the security basis for one-time signature unforgeability?

options.text: Discrete logarithm hardness
Preimage resistance of the hash function
The birthday paradox
Integer factorization hardness

questions.explanation: To forge an OTS signature on a new message, an attacker needs secret values that weren't revealed. Finding these requires computing a preimage: given pk = H(sk), find sk. This requires breaking preimage resistance.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of basic one-time signatures like Lamport signatures?

options.text: They are not quantum-resistant
They have very large key and signature sizes
Verification is computationally expensive
They require special hardware

questions.explanation: Lamport signatures require two hash-sized values per bit of the message hash. For 256-bit security, this means keys of ~32KB and signatures of ~16KB - much larger than RSA or ECDSA.

questions.question: How do Merkle trees enable signing multiple messages with one public key?

options.text: By using a different hash function for each message
By pre-generating many OTS key pairs and committing to them via a tree root
By making the OTS scheme reusable
By compressing the signatures

questions.explanation: Merkle trees pre-generate 2^h OTS key pairs and build a hash tree over their public keys. The tree root serves as a single public key. Each signature includes the OTS signature plus a path from the leaf to the root for verification.

questions.question: What distinguishes SPHINCS+ (SLH-DSA) from XMSS/LMS?

options.text: SPHINCS+ is faster
SPHINCS+ has smaller signatures
SPHINCS+ is stateless (no state management required)
SPHINCS+ uses lattice problems

questions.explanation: SPHINCS+ (standardized as SLH-DSA in FIPS 205) is stateless - it doesn't need to track which keys have been used. XMSS/LMS require careful state management to prevent key reuse. The tradeoff is larger signatures for SPHINCS+.

questions.question: Collision resistance requires O(2^n) operations to break for an n-bit hash function.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Due to the birthday paradox, collision resistance only provides O(2^(n/2)) security. Finding any two messages with the same hash requires about 2^(n/2) operations, not 2^n. This is why we need 256-bit hashes for 128-bit collision resistance.
